learning_objectives: Explain the difference between additive (RGB) and subtractive (CMYK) color models
Apply color harmony principles (complementary, analogous, triadic) to digital compositions
Use HSB/HSL color space to make precise adjustments to hue, saturation, and brightness
readings: Color and Light by James Gurney, Ch. 1-4
Interaction of Color by Josef Albers, Exercises 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Digital Art Fundamentals. This course teaches the visual principles and technical skills you will need to create game art, UI elements, and digital illustrations. We begin with the foundation that underpins every visual decision: color.
key_concepts: Additive color (RGB): red, green, blue light combining to white; the model used by screens
Subtractive color (CMYK): cyan, magenta, yellow, key combining to black; the model used in print
Color harmonies: complementary (opposite on the wheel), analogous (adjacent), triadic (equidistant)
HSB/HSL color space: Hue (the color), Saturation (intensity), Brightness/Lightness (value)
Color temperature: warm (red/orange/yellow) versus cool (blue/green/violet) and its emotional impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a game environment color palette using Adobe Color CC, then applying it to a simple scene composition in Photoshop
setup_requirements: Adobe Photoshop or free alternative (Krita, GIMP)
Graphics tablet recommended but not required

review.value: Screens use RGB (additive, light-based, white is all colors); print uses CMYK (subtractive, ink-based, black is all colors). Game art is always RGB.
Systematic relationships on the color wheel that produce visually pleasing combinations; critical for readable game UI and cohesive environment art

learning_objectives: Apply the rule of thirds, golden ratio, and leading lines to digital compositions
Create visual hierarchy using size, contrast, color, and position
Analyze how composition guides the viewer's eye in game screenshots and UI layouts
readings: Framed Ink by Marcos Mateu-Mestre, Ch. 1-3
Design Basics Index by Jim Krause, Composition section

lecture_notes.introduction: A technically skilled artist who cannot compose an image leaves the viewer lost. Composition is the invisible architecture that directs attention and communicates meaning.
key_concepts: Rule of thirds: dividing the canvas into a 3x3 grid and placing focal points at intersections
Golden ratio / golden spiral: a naturally occurring proportion (~1.618:1) found in art and nature
Leading lines: paths in the image that direct the viewer's eye toward the focal point
Visual hierarchy: the arrangement of elements so the most important content is perceived first
Negative space: empty areas that give the composition room to breathe and emphasize subjects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Overlaying composition grids on game screenshots (Journey, Hollow Knight, Ori and the Blind Forest) to show rule-of-thirds and leading-line usage
setup_requirements: Image editing software with grid overlay capability
Collection of game screenshots for analysis

review.value: The deliberate arrangement of elements by importance using size, contrast, color, and position so that the viewer processes the most critical information first
Intentionally empty areas in a composition that prevent clutter, create focus, and allow the viewer's eye to rest between elements

learning_objectives: Configure brush settings for line work, shading, and texture in a raster editor
Use layers, blend modes, and masks effectively
Apply a structured digital painting workflow: sketch, line art, flat color, shading, detail
readings: Digital Painting Techniques by 3DTotal Publishing, Ch. 1-2
Beginner's Guide to Digital Painting in Photoshop by 3DTotal, Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: Knowing your tools is as important as knowing your theory. Today we cover the practical skills of digital drawing, from brush configuration to a professional painting workflow.
key_concepts: Raster vs. vector: pixels versus mathematical paths and when to use each
Brush dynamics: size, opacity, flow, pressure sensitivity, and custom brush creation
Layer management: naming conventions, groups, clipping masks, blend modes (Multiply, Screen, Overlay)
Non-destructive editing: adjustment layers, smart objects, layer masks
Structured workflow: thumbnail, rough sketch, clean line art, flat colors, lighting pass, detail pass
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live digital painting of a game character from rough sketch to finished illustration using the structured workflow
setup_requirements: Adobe Photoshop, Krita, or Clip Studio Paint installed
Custom brush pack (provided on course page)

review.value: Mathematical operations that control how a layer's pixels interact with layers below; Multiply darkens, Screen lightens, Overlay boosts contrast
Techniques (masks, adjustment layers, smart objects) that allow changes without permanently altering original pixel data

learning_objectives: Create readable pixel art sprites at common game resolutions (16x16, 32x32, 64x64)
Apply dithering, anti-aliasing, and limited palette techniques
Animate a pixel art character using a basic walk cycle
readings: Pixel Logic by Michael Azzi, Ch. 1-4
The Spriters Resource tutorials on animation fundamentals

lecture_notes.introduction: Pixel art is not merely nostalgia; it is a discipline of extreme economy. Every pixel must earn its place. Today we learn the craft of making small images communicate clearly.
key_concepts: Resolution and scale: choosing a sprite size based on game resolution and screen density
Outline techniques: full outline, selective outline (sel-out), no outline
Dithering: simulating gradients with patterned pixel placement when palette is limited
Anti-aliasing by hand: placing intermediate-color pixels at jagged edges
Animation principles for pixel art: walk cycles, squash and stretch at low resolution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a 32x32 character sprite in Aseprite, then animating a 4-frame walk cycle
setup_requirements: Aseprite or Piskel (free web-based pixel editor)
Grid-based reference sheet for common sprite proportions

review.value: A technique of alternating pixels of two colors in a pattern to simulate a third, intermediate color; essential when working with limited palettes
Using colored outlines that match adjacent fill areas instead of a uniform black outline, producing a softer, more integrated look at small resolutions

learning_objectives: Create scalable vector game assets using Bezier curves and boolean operations
Apply typographic principles (hierarchy, legibility, pairing) to game UI text
Export vector assets appropriately for game engine integration
readings: Vector Basic Training by Von Glitschka, Ch. 1-5
Thinking with Type by Ellen Lupton, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Vector graphics give you resolution-independent assets that scale to any screen size. Combined with solid typography, they form the backbone of clean, professional game UI.
key_concepts: Bezier curves: anchor points, handles, and the math behind smooth curves
Boolean operations: union, subtract, intersect, exclude for shape construction
Stroke versus fill: when to use each and how to expand strokes for game export
Typography fundamentals: serif vs. sans-serif, x-height, kerning, leading, tracking
Font pairing for games: a display font for titles, a readable font for body text, and a monospace font for stats/data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a set of game UI icons (health, mana, inventory, settings) in Adobe Illustrator using boolean operations, then pairing fonts for a game menu mockup
setup_requirements: Access to Google Fonts or similar free font library

review.value: Mathematical curves defined by anchor points and control handles; the foundation of all vector graphics and scalable game assets
Using size, weight, color, and spacing to create a clear reading order so players can scan game UI quickly under time pressure

class_schedule.topic: UI Design Basics and Asset Pipelines
learning_objectives: Design a functional game UI layout following platform conventions
Create a sprite sheet and texture atlas for efficient game engine loading
Set up an asset pipeline from source files to engine-ready exports
readings: Game UI Design by Don L. Daglow, online article series
Texture Atlas and Sprite Sheet Best Practices (Unity Documentation)

lecture_notes.introduction: Beautiful art is useless if it does not reach the game engine in the right format at the right size. Today we connect the creative process to the technical pipeline.
key_concepts: Game UI layout patterns: HUD, menus, modals, tooltips, and responsive scaling
Platform conventions: mobile touch targets (minimum 44x44 pt), console safe zones, PC flexibility
Sprite sheets: arranging multiple sprites in a single image for batch rendering
Texture atlases: packing diverse assets into a single texture to reduce draw calls
Asset pipeline: source file (PSD/AI) to export (PNG/SVG) to engine import settings (compression, filtering, atlas packing)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete game HUD mockup in Figma, exporting assets to a sprite sheet using TexturePacker, and importing into Unity with correct settings
setup_requirements: Figma or equivalent UI design tool
TexturePacker (free trial) or equivalent sprite sheet tool

review.value: A single image containing multiple smaller images packed together; reduces GPU draw calls and improves rendering performance by batching sprites
The complete workflow from creating art in a source tool through export, optimization, and import into the game engine with correct settings

quizzes.topic: Color Theory, Composition, and Digital Drawing Fundamentals

questions.question: A game designer asks you to create a color palette for a horror game set in an abandoned hospital. Describe which color harmony you would use, which color temperature would dominate, and explain how you would use saturation to reinforce the game's mood.
questions.answer: An analogous harmony centered on cool blue-greens and desaturated teals would establish an eerie, clinical atmosphere. Cool temperature dominates because it evokes isolation, sterility, and unease. Saturation should be kept very low across the environment (washed out, institutional colors) with one or two highly saturated accent colors (a vivid red for blood, danger, or interactive objects) to create visual shock and draw attention to critical gameplay elements. The contrast between the desaturated world and the rare saturated accent maximizes both mood and readability.

rubric.key: Identifies appropriate color harmony with reasoning
Explains color temperature choice and its emotional effect
Describes saturation strategy for mood and gameplay readability

questions.question: Explain the difference between a raster image and a vector image. For each, give one game art use case where that format is the better choice and explain why.
questions.answer: A raster image is composed of a grid of pixels; it has a fixed resolution and loses quality when scaled up. A vector image is defined by mathematical paths (Bezier curves) and can scale to any resolution without quality loss. Raster is better for detailed character illustrations and environment paintings where subtle gradients, textures, and photographic detail are needed (e.g., a painted background in Hollow Knight). Vector is better for UI elements like icons, buttons, and logos that must render crisply across multiple screen resolutions and device sizes (e.g., a mobile game's menu icons that must look sharp on both phones and tablets).

rubric.key: Correct definitions of raster and vector
Appropriate raster use case with reasoning
Appropriate vector use case with reasoning

quizzes.topic: Pixel Art, Vector Graphics, and Typography

questions.question: You are creating a 16x16 pixel art character for a top-down RPG. At this resolution, you have 256 total pixels to work with. Explain two specific techniques you would use to make the character readable and recognizable despite the extreme size constraint.
questions.answer: Technique 1: High-contrast silhouette. At 16x16, internal detail is nearly impossible to read, so the character must be recognizable by outline alone. Using a dark outline against a lighter fill, with the silhouette showing a distinctive feature (a hat, a weapon, or a cape), ensures the character reads clearly even at 1:1 pixel scale. Technique 2: Limited palette with maximum value contrast. Using 4-6 carefully chosen colors with strong value differences (light skin against dark armor, bright hair against muted background) ensures each body part is distinguishable. Every pixel of color boundary becomes a shape-defining edge, so high value contrast between adjacent regions is essential for readability.

rubric.key: First technique is specific to low-resolution constraints
Second technique is specific and distinct from the first
Explains why each technique works at this resolution

questions.question: In game typography, why is it important to pair a display font with a highly legible body font? Give an example of a poor pairing and explain what makes it fail.
questions.answer: Display fonts (decorative, expressive) attract attention and establish mood for titles and headers, but they sacrifice legibility at small sizes or in long passages. A body font must be highly legible because players read ability descriptions, dialogue, tutorials, and item stats under time pressure. Pairing them correctly means each font serves its role without competing. A poor pairing example: using a blackletter (gothic) display font for the game title alongside a similarly decorative script font for body text. Both fonts have low legibility due to ornate letterforms, and their visual weight and complexity compete rather than contrast. The result is a UI that is difficult to read, especially on small screens, and lacks visual hierarchy because neither font clearly signals 'read me first.'

rubric.key: Explains the functional role of display vs. body fonts
Specific poor pairing example with named font categories
Explains why the pairing fails (legibility, competition, hierarchy)

major_assignments.title: Game Art Style Guide and Asset Set
major_assignments.description: Create a comprehensive art style guide for an original game concept, including a defined color palette, typography selections, UI component designs, and a set of game-ready assets (character sprites, environment tiles, and UI elements) that demonstrate consistent application of the style guide across all asset types.
objectives: Define and document a cohesive visual style for a game concept
Apply color theory, composition, and typography principles to practical asset creation
Create production-ready assets in at least two formats (pixel art or painted raster, plus vector UI)
Demonstrate a professional asset pipeline from source files to engine-ready exports
deliverables: Style guide document (4-6 pages) covering color palette (with hex codes), typography selections (with usage rules), art direction references, and technical specifications
Character sprite sheet: at least one character with idle and walk animations (pixel art at 32x32 or painted at higher resolution)
Environment tile set: at least 12 tileable pieces that combine to form a playable area
UI kit: menu screen mockup, HUD overlay, and at least 6 icons, all in vector format
Exported texture atlas with all assets packed and a JSON metadata file mapping sprite regions

criteria: Color palette is cohesive and appropriate for the stated game genre/mood
Typography selections are legible and well-paired
Technical specifications are precise enough for another artist to follow
Art direction references support the stated visual goals
Character is readable and recognizable at intended display size
Animation frames are smooth and follow basic animation principles
Style is consistent with the documented style guide
Technical execution demonstrates skill with the chosen medium
Tiles connect seamlessly without visible seams or alignment errors
Tile set provides enough variety to create a visually interesting area
Color and style are consistent with the character art and style guide
Tiles demonstrate understanding of visual depth and readability
UI mockup follows platform conventions and typographic hierarchy
Icons are clear, consistent in style, and readable at small sizes
All assets are exported in correct formats with appropriate compression
Texture atlas is properly packed with valid metadata
Cohesive art style consistently applied across all asset types
Strong application of color theory, composition, and typography principles
Production-quality assets that are technically ready for engine import
Professional documentation that another artist could follow

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of visual theory knowledge including color, composition, typography, and format considerations

in_class_exercises.description: Hands-on exercises completed during class sessions covering digital drawing, pixel art, and vector techniques

style_guide_and_asset_set.description: Comprehensive art style guide with a full set of game-ready assets demonstrating consistent visual direction

portfolio_pieces.description: Three individual portfolio pieces (one painted illustration, one pixel art scene, one vector UI kit) demonstrating range

final_critique_presentation.description: Presentation of a complete body of work with peer and instructor critique, demonstrating growth and artistic voice

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is required for all sessions. Studio time and critique sessions cannot be replicated outside class. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade by one half-letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day. Art critique deadlines are firm because peer review depends on all work being available simultaneously.
course_policies.collaboration: Artistic inspiration from others is natural but all submitted work must be your own creation. Tracing or copying another artist's work without explicit permission and disclosure constitutes academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted art must be original. Use of AI image generators for submitted work is prohibited. Reference images must be cited. Stock assets may not be submitted as original work.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to a graphics tablet with pressure sensitivity and at least one raster editor (Photoshop, Krita, or Clip Studio Paint) and one vector editor (Illustrator, Inkscape, or Figma). Lab computers with all software are available during open studio hours.
